title: Cloud AI Services for Working Professionals

modules.title: The Cloud AI Landscape: How AWS, Azure, and GCP Have Divided the Market
modules.sub: Mapping the strategic origins and strengths of each platform
modules.description: Learners survey how AWS, Azure, and GCP each arrived at their current AI positions through distinct corporate histories and strategic priorities. They examine market share data, flagship AI bets, and ecosystem philosophies to build a baseline mental model. By the end, they can articulate the key differentiators that make each platform attractive to different types of organizations.

modules.title: Core Managed AI Services: Vision, Language, Speech, and Prediction APIs
modules.sub: Comparing out-of-the-box AI APIs across all three clouds
modules.description: Learners conduct a structured side-by-side evaluation of pre-built managed services for vision, natural language, speech, and tabular prediction across AWS, Azure, and GCP. They assess accuracy benchmarks, ease of integration, pricing tiers, and service maturity for each capability category. The module concludes with a hands-on comparison exercise that surfaces which platform leads in each domain for a given use case type.

modules.title: Foundation Model Access and Fine-Tuning on Each Platform
modules.sub: Evaluating how each cloud exposes and customizes large models
modules.description: Learners explore the foundation model catalogs available through Amazon Bedrock, Azure OpenAI Service, and Google Vertex AI Model Garden, noting which third-party and proprietary models each platform surfaces. They compare fine-tuning workflows, context window limits, and prompt management tooling across the three environments. Decision criteria around model choice, customization depth, and commercial licensing are applied to a realistic organizational scenario.

modules.title: MLOps Pipelines: SageMaker, Azure ML, and Vertex AI Compared
modules.sub: Assessing end-to-end ML lifecycle tooling on each cloud
modules.description: Learners trace a machine learning workload from data preparation through model deployment and monitoring using SageMaker, Azure ML, and Vertex AI. They evaluate each platform's pipeline abstractions, experiment tracking, registry management, and inference serving options against a common rubric. The module produces a comparative scorecard learners can adapt to their own team's MLOps maturity and tooling preferences.

modules.title: Cost Modeling, Vendor Lock-In, and Multi-Cloud AI Strategy
modules.sub: Quantifying financial risk and architectural freedom in cloud AI
modules.description: Learners build a cost model for a representative AI workload on each platform, accounting for compute, API call volume, storage, and egress fees. They analyze the technical and contractual mechanisms that create vendor lock-in and evaluate multi-cloud and hybrid strategies for mitigating that risk. The module challenges learners to weigh cost predictability against architectural flexibility when making a platform commitment.

modules.title: Security, Compliance, and Data Residency for Enterprise AI Workloads
modules.sub: Navigating regulatory and governance requirements across platforms
modules.description: Learners examine how AWS, Azure, and GCP each address enterprise requirements around identity and access management, data encryption, audit logging, and compliance certifications relevant to AI workloads. They apply a governance checklist to assess each platform's data residency controls and sovereign cloud options. The module equips learners to identify which platform best satisfies the regulatory constraints of a specific industry or geography.

modules.title: Selecting the Right Platform for Your Use Case: A Decision Framework
modules.sub: Applying a structured rubric to justify a platform recommendation
modules.description: Learners are introduced to a weighted decision framework that integrates technical capability, cost, compliance, MLOps maturity, and strategic risk into a single evaluation structure. They practice applying the framework to several representative professional scenarios before turning it toward their own organizational context. The output is a documented, defensible platform recommendation grounded in the framework rather than vendor preference.

modules.title: Architecting a Cloud AI Solution for a Professional Scenario
modules.sub: Designing a complete cloud AI architecture for a real-world brief
modules.description: Learners receive a detailed professional scenario and design a full cloud AI architecture using the platform they selected in the previous module, specifying services, data flows, security controls, and MLOps tooling. They document tradeoffs, flag risks, and outline a migration or scaling path for the future state. The completed architecture diagram and written justification serve as the course's capstone deliverable demonstrating mastery of the evaluation and design process.

synopsis: AWS, Azure, and GCP each offer mature but divergent AI service ecosystems â and professionals making platform decisions without a comparative framework are routinely locked into suboptimal choices. This course gives technical leads, architects, and decision-makers an honest, side-by-side analysis of capabilities, tradeoffs, and strategic considerations across all three major cloud AI platforms.
rationale: Cloud AI platform selection has multi-year cost and capability implications, yet most professionals default to whichever cloud their organization already uses rather than evaluating fit for AI-specific workloads.
learning_outcome: Learners will be able to evaluate AWS, Azure, and GCP AI services against a structured decision framework and produce a justified platform recommendation for a specific organizational AI workload.
source_signals: Building a Virtual Machine Inside ChatGPT (score: 4803)
nearest_existing: Every Age Believes Its Tool Is the Last Tool
